region_of_origin: Central America; widely cultivated in South and Southeast Asia including Singapore, Malaysia, and India

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium chiku fruit (approximately 100g edible flesh) is a typical single serving, commonly consumed fresh as a snack or dessert fruit in Singapore and South Asia.

nutrient_source.title: Chiku (Sapodilla) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chiku (F04010008). Values for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, sugar, and select vitamins supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 09322, Sapodilla, raw) where HPB does not report these fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for sapodilla/chiku
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chiku (sapodilla) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on the closest tropical fruit entries with comparable sugar (~16g/100g) and fibre (~5g/100g) profiles in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 19.96) / 100 ≈ 11.0 per 100g serving. GI of 55 is considered 'low' according to the University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI < 55 is low, 55-69 is medium, >70 is high).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (12mg/100g) and moderate potassium (193mg/100g) with high dietary fibre (5.3g/100g) supporting vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Chiku is suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed as whole fruit (not juice) to retain fibre; limit to one medium fruit per sitting to manage total natural sugar intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High natural sugar (~16g/100g) with low estimated GI (55) partially offset by substantial dietary fibre (5.3g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit chiku to half a fruit (approximately 50g) per serving, pair with a protein or healthy fat source, and monitor individual postprandial glucose response given the low GI but relatively high natural sugar content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.19g/100g), zero trans fat, high dietary fibre (5.3g/100g), and antioxidant vitamin C (14.7mg/100g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chiku is appropriate for individuals managing cardiovascular disease as a whole-fruit snack; its high fibre content supports LDL reduction and its low saturated fat profile poses no atherogenic risk at typical serving sizes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant vitamin C (14.7mg/100g) and high dietary fibre (5.3g/100g) supporting anti-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chiku may be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its vitamin C and fibre content support anti-inflammatory pathways, but high natural sugar should be moderated to avoid excess inflammatory mediator promotion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chiku (also known as sapodilla, ciku, or sapota; scientific name Manilkara zapota) is a sweet tropical fruit native to Central America and widely cultivated across South and Southeast Asia, including Singapore, Malaysia, and India. The fruit has a rough, brown, potato-like skin and a soft, grainy, caramel-brown flesh with a texture often described as sandy or granular, similar to a ripe pear. Its flavour is distinctively sweet, reminiscent of brown sugar, caramel, or malt, with subtle notes of vanilla. Chiku is consumed fresh as a snack or dessert fruit and is popular in Singapore hawker culture and South Asian households. Nutritionally, chiku provides 83 kcal per 100g, with 19.96g of carbohydrates (of which approximately 16g is natural sugar), 5.3g of dietary fibre, 0.44g of protein, and 1.1g of fat. Its high fibre content supports digestive health and moderates glycaemic response. Sodium is very low at 12mg/100g, making it suitable for hypertension management. The estimated glycemic index is 55 (low), and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 50g per serving. Chiku also provides vitamin C (14.7mg/100g), potassium (193mg/100g), calcium (21mg/100g), and iron (0.8mg/100g), contributing to antioxidant defence, vascular health, and bone support.
common_user_queries: What are the health benefits of chiku?
How many calories in a chiku?
What is the glycemic index of sapodilla?
What is the difference between chiku and sapodilla?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match, F04010008). Secondary micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 09322, Sapodilla, raw), an approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, clearly documented. All values are internally consistent and plausible for fresh sapodilla.
notes: Sugar_g value of 16.0g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 09322) as HPB does not separately report sugar; this is consistent with the total carbohydrate of 19.96g and dietary fibre of 5.3g. Saturated fat (0.19g), monounsaturated_fat (0.52g), polyunsaturated_fat (0.01g), and micronutrient values (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 09322 (Sapodilla, raw) as HPB does not report these fields. Trans fat is reported as 0.0g (confirmed negligible for fresh whole fruit per USDA). Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are 0.0g/mcg as confirmed for plant-based whole fruit with no animal-derived components. Cholesterol is 0.0mg as confirmed for plant-based fruit. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chiku/sapodilla appears in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. According to the University of Sydney GI Database classification, a GI of 55 is considered 'low'. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are best-available matches for sapodilla whole fruit; SNOMED CT code 415562000 is the closest available substance code. Chiku is a Singaporean and South Asian fruit; multilingual names provided in Chinese (人心果), Malay (Ciku), Tamil (சப்போட்டா), and Hindi (चीकू) per G6 Singaporean food multilingual requirements.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to accurately reflect the glycemic_index of 55, as per the University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI < 55 is low, 55-69 is medium, >70 is high). Updated gi_source notes to clarify this classification.
